1.1: The warrant is the crew's chief weapon — Bole produces the Lord's seal the way other men draw steel, and it turns robbery into a lawful levy. A stranger who challenges the paper is made to be challenging the Lord himself; that threat is most of what keeps the village still. Blades are worn openly by the crew and set aside by the cowed; the crew is armed, idle, and does not expect to have to fight.
1.2: The crew is SIX men and NOT one loyal unit — two are the Lord's (Bole, Vane), one the Master's (Pell), three are hired muscle (Cob and two hard-cases). Read each man's disposition from the place's prose and play him honestly: Bole weighs his comfort, Vane does a fight's arithmetic and leaves if the odds turn, Pell sells anyone to save his ledger and his skin, Cob is the weak seam who breaks if split off, the hard-cases hold no loyalty past the next cup. Their unity is a thing to be broken, not a wall to be stormed.
1._: The occupation — how the crew holds Brackenfoot. Force is mostly threatened, not spent: the warrant, the warning, the example made on the Green. A newcomer is warned off first — a local matter, the Lord's business, move along — and only meddling turns the warning into violence. What the crew IS depends on which man; the talk is grubby and human and has no substance behind it, and is not to be dignified with depth.

content.2: The village is cowed, watching, and compromised — three weeks of fear have taught it to keep its head down and to hate itself for it. Strangers are warned off most insistently of all by Reeve Haldan, who is the one left holding whatever the party does. The villagers are frightened, sometimes petty, not noble peasants — a party must decide they are worth it anyway. Grain and goods are the crew's to seize; the village dares not resist openly until something breaks it, and it is one cruel word from breaking.

3.1: THE REINFORCEMENT CLOCK — the single largest fact of the scenario. If a rider gets out the Slip and down to Lowfen, the Lord's thirty men and a hard captain come back inside THREE DAYS and burn Brackenfoot to the door-sills and call it order. Stop the rider at the Slip or in the Long Wood and it is a fair fight against six; let one clear the ford and the burn-clock is live — a day-grain window counting down, and the scenario turns to defend or evacuate. Everything the party does is measured against whether a rider gets out.
3.2: THE CREW DRINKS EVERY NIGHT and is useless after dark — its watch is one dozing hard-case at best. After dark, and only against the crew's negligence, an act of stealth, movement, or approach takes −2 to its difficulty: here the dark is the party's ally, because the men holding the village have stopped guarding it. By day the crew is bored but present and the opening closes. The night is the single strongest move on the board.
3._: The clock and the dark — the tactical spine. Two facts set the scenario's shape: whether a rider reaches the keep (the three-day burn-clock), and that the crew guards badly after dark. A party that grasps these beats six men; a party that kicks the door in daylight with the Slip open behind it does not.

content.4: The diggings and the pale glass-stone — never explained. The men are driven to cut stone that is counted and carted up to the keep, and no one in Brackenfoot knows what it is for. Leave it cold. The one wrong note is Pell, who answers not to the Lord but to the Master, and says I'll tell the Master in a voice that makes even Bole go quiet. Never explain the Master; no one in Brackenfoot can. It is the single cold thread in an otherwise grubby, human scenario, and it follows the party up the valley only if Pell lives and runs.

_.1: Perception — the village is open ground: the Green is seen from every cottage door, and anyone crossing it is watched from all sides even when no one seems to look. Sight needs light — the Sow is smoke-dark, the lanes unlit after dark. Sound carries between the close cottages; a raised voice on the Green is heard through every shutter. The wood and the high slopes swallow both sight and sound.
_.2: Conflict — the medium judges from character capability (Character Force, read from the passport, not invented), position, and physical reality. The crew is competent in a brawl and lazy on watch; a public act on the Green warns the whole village at once, a quiet act in the dark need not. Direct contact — a drawn blade, a grabbed arm, a blocked door — forces a resolution now; a word, a glance, an entrance accumulate as events. Dice (rules:nomad) resolve the genuinely uncertain; they never make the impossible happen, and never turn the crew's justifications into more than noise.
_.3: Pacing — characters acting at different speeds coexist; the quick produce more events, the slow take them in on their next commit. The scenario's own beats — the squeeze, the trigger at the Well, the stocks, the killing in the square — fire in sequence or when the party's poking sets them off. The pressure is already high the morning the party arrives.
_._: Rules constraining action in and around Brackenfoot. Perception is physical: the open village is watched from every door, sound carries between close walls, the dark and the wood hide what daylight shows. Conflict resolves by honest narrative judgment — competence, position, and physical reality decide outcomes; NOMAD's numbers (rules:nomad) sharpen contested moments but never override what would plainly happen, and never make the crew's thin words into more than the noise men make while taking things. One contribution covers one natural beat — seconds in a scuffle, a minute in talk, longer on the road. No turns, no initiative: when you commit is itself a choice.
